back_translation: And He began to speak to them in a parable: 'A man planted a vineyard, put a fence around it, dug a wine vat, built a watchtower, then leased it to vinedressers, and went away to a foreign land.
When the harvest season came, he sent a slave to the vinedressers to receive a share of the fruit of the vineyard from them.
But they seized that slave, beat him, and sent him back empty-handed.
He sent another slave to them; that one they struck on the head and dishonored.
He sent yet another; that one they killed; and there were many others — some they beat, some they killed.
He still had one more, his beloved son. Finally he sent that son to them, thinking: 'They will respect my son.'
But those vinedressers said among themselves: 'This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and the inheritance will be ours.'
So they seized the son, killed him, and threw the body outside the vineyard.
What will the owner of the vineyard do next? He will come and destroy those vinedressers, and give the vineyard to others.
Have you not read this Scripture? 'The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone.
This is from the Lord, and is marvelous in our eyes.'
The leaders sought an opportunity to seize Him, but feared the crowd, because they knew He had spoken this parable against them; so they left Him and went away.
Afterwards they sent some Pharisees and some Herodians to Him, in order to catch Him in His words.

And when they came, they said: 'Teacher, we know You are upright and do not defer to anyone, for You show no partiality, but teach the way of God in truth. Is it lawful according to the Law to pay tribute tax to Caesar or not? Should we pay or not pay?'
But He knew their hypocrisy, so He said to them: 'Why do you test me? Bring me a denarius coin to look at.'
They brought one, and He asked them: 'Whose image and inscription is this?' They said: 'Caesar's.'
Jesus said to them: 'The things of Caesar, render to Caesar; and the things of God, render to God.' And they were greatly amazed at Him.
Then some Sadducees, who say there is no resurrection from the dead, came to Him and asked Him,

'Teacher, Moses wrote a commandment to us: If anyone's brother dies, having a wife but leaving no child, his younger brother shall take the wife of the deceased to be his wife and raise up offspring for his brother.
Suppose there were seven brothers; the eldest took a wife, but when he died, there was no offspring.
The second took her as wife and died without leaving offspring; and the third the same.
All seven left no offspring. Last of all, the woman also died.
At the resurrection on the day they rise, whose wife will she be? For all seven had her as wife.'
Jesus answered them: 'Are you not in error for this reason — that you do not know the Scriptures, nor the power of God?
For when people rise from the dead, they do not marry nor are given in marriage, but are like the angels in heaven.

And concerning the dead being raised, have you not read in the book of Moses, in the passage about the bush, where God said to Moses: 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob'?
He is not the God of the dead, but of the living. You are very far in error.'
And one scribe heard them debating, and seeing that He answered well, came forward and asked Him: 'Which commandment is chief above all the commandments?'
Jesus answered: 'The chief is: Hear, O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one,
and you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your mind, and with all your strength.'
The second is: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself.' There is no other commandment greater than these two.'
The scribe said to Him: 'Teacher, You have spoken well according to the truth that God is one, and there is no other besides Him,

and to love Him with all the heart, with all the understanding, and with all the strength, and to love one's neighbor as oneself, is far more important than all burnt offerings and animal sacrifices.'
Jesus saw that he answered with understanding, so He said to him: 'You are not far from the kingdom of God.' After that no one dared question Him any further.
While Jesus was teaching in the temple courts, He raised the question: 'Why do the scribes say that the Christ is the son of David?
David himself said by the Holy Spirit: 'The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand, until I subdue your enemies under your feet.'
David himself calls Him Lord; then how can He be his son?' And the great crowd listened to Him with delight.
And in His teaching, He said: 'Beware of the scribes who like to walk around showing off in long robes, who love greetings in the marketplaces,
the chief seats in the synagogues, and the chief places at banquets,
who devour widows' houses and pray long prayers for show. These will receive heavier judgment.'
While He was sitting opposite the offering box, He watched the crowd putting copper coins into the offering box, and many rich people put in large amounts.
Then a poor widow came and put in two small copper coins, which are worth one quadrans.
He called His disciples and said to them: 'I tell you the truth: this poor widow has given more than all those who put into the offering box.
Because all of them put in from their surplus wealth, but this woman gave from her poverty — everything she had, all she had to live on.'
